docs.text: How I Do Research (Working Playbook) This document exists to reduce friction, not to impose discipline. Research sessions should start cleanly, end safely, and leave evidence behind. This is a living document. Core Principles Research happens in sessions, not moods Confusion is allowed; loss of state is not Everything important must survive power loss, human error, and future-me Streaming adds cognitive load — compensate explicitly Before a Research Session (5–10 minutes) Declare intent Write one sentence answering: What am I trying to figure out right now? This can go in: - a research log - a scratchpad - the stream description - a comment at the top of a notebook If I can’t state intent, I’m not ready to start. Establish scope Define: - what is in scope for this session - what is explicitly out of scope This prevents productive-looking wandering. Prep the environment Open only the tools relevant to the session Close unrelated tabs Start recording only if this session benefits from being streamed If streaming: - say the intent out loud - accept that pauses and mistakes are part of the artifact During a Research Session Think out loud (especially if streaming) Narration forces clarity. Confusion spoken aloud is often halfway solved. Capture decisions, not everything Record: - assumptions - choices - changes in direction - things that didn’t work and why Do not attempt to transcribe reality. Leave breadcrumbs If you stop mid-thought: - leave a TODO - write a one-line “next step” - add a comment explaining current state Future-me should never have to reverse-engineer intent. Reading for Literature Review (Integrate existing notes here) Guiding principles: - read to map the field, not to memorize papers - track claims, methods, assumptions, and what each paper is for - write summaries in my own words immediately Writing (Lightweight Guidance) Writing usually comes naturally, but structure still helps. When to write as soon as an idea stabilizes before the research feels “done” Drafts are thinking tools, not deliverables. How to write productively start ugly write explanations as if to an intelligent outsider prefer clarity over cleverness use headings early; polish later If stuck: - write the section I don’t want to write - explain the problem instead of the solution Ending a Research Session (Non-Negotiable) This is the most important part. Write a closing note Answer: - what did I learn? - what changed? - what is the next obvious step? One paragraph is enough. Commit meaningful changes If files changed in a way that affects meaning: git add . git commit -m "Describe what changed and why" Commits are semantic checkpoints, not perfection markers. Backup Run the backup process. Do not defer this. Backups should be: - encrypted - automated where possible - boring If I’m too tired to back up, I’m too tired to stop safely. Clean exit Stop recording Close tools Leave workspace in a known-good state Research sessions should end intentionally, not by exhaustion. Streaming-Specific Notes Streams are secondary artifacts Clarity beats performance Pauses and dead ends are allowed Narrate uncertainty honestly If a session shouldn’t be streamed, don’t force it. Maintenance Revisit this document: - when friction appears - after major workflow changes - when something breaks The process exists to serve the work, not the other way around.

docs.text: The Wheel Turns: Analyzing the Lifecycle of Innovation, Adoption, and Commercialization Abstract A concise summary that contextualizes the cycle, introduces the core themes, and mentions the study’s contribution to understanding technology’s lifecycle in terms of social and economic impacts. Keywords Technology Adoption, Commercialization, Community Culture, Innovation Lifecycle, Early Adopters, Social Media Commercialization, Tech Communities. Introduction The evolution of technology often mirrors a predictable cycle: innovation emerges, gains a dedicated following, peaks, and then undergoes a shift toward commercialization that ultimately alters or dilutes its original purpose. This cycle—a near inevitability in modern technology—brings significant shifts in community culture, user experience, and platform objectives. As platforms grow from niche spaces to corporate giants, user autonomy is often diminished, while monetization, surveillance, and corporate interests dominate. This paper examines the lifecycle of technology through case studies: 1) IRC as an early online community, 2) the transition from MySpace to Facebook and Instagram in social media, 3) the rise of smartphones from PDAs to devices deeply integrated with surveillance infrastructures, 4) the fall of Twitter; and 5) the fall of Youtube. These examples showcase the repetitive nature of technological adoption, commercialization, and the recurring trade-offs between innovation and profit. Here, the analysis explores not only the predictable phases of technological growth but also questions if the cycle could, or should, be broken to preserve the original intent and value of innovation. Each case demonstrates how community needs, user privacy, and platform autonomy often become secondary concerns in the pursuit of growth and revenue. While the cycle of commercialization is common, some platforms manage to resist these pressures. Signal and Wikipedia stand as exceptions, structured deliberately to avoid the pitfalls of commercialization. Through contrasting these “outliers” with more commercialized platforms, this paper examines what makes resistance possible and how these alternative structures might influence the trajectory of tech innovation. Literature Review Objective: Position your argument within existing scholarship on the adoption of technology, the role of early adopters, and the influence of commercialization. Case Studies: The Lifecycle of Innovation, Adoption, and Commercialization in Tech 2. Social Media: MySpace to Facebook to Instagram Structure: Early Social Media Platforms – MySpace : Outline MySpace’s rise as an innovative social network and its appeal to youth culture and creatives. Highlight its unregulated design, user-driven customization, and music-centric features. Mainstream Success and Commercialization – Facebook : Examine Facebook’s early exclusivity and eventual mass adoption. Discuss how Facebook shifted social media towards a data-driven, algorithmic model, introducing targeted ads, privacy trade-offs, and commercial incentives. The Rise of Instagram and Social Media’s Maturity : Discuss Instagram’s design, focusing on visuals and mobile-friendly features, which allowed it to capitalize on smartphone adoption. Detail its acquisition by Facebook and how it became part of the platform’s commercialized ecosystem. Social Media’s Commercialization Cycle : Compare each platform’s phases, noting the progression from niche interest to widespread commercial adoption, followed by shifts in user culture and platform design. Recommended Reading: "The Facebook Effect: The Inside Story of the Company That Is Connecting the World" by David Kirkpatrick. Chronicles Facebook’s journey, cultural impact, and monetization strategies. "It’s Complicated: The Social Lives of Networked Teens" by danah boyd. Offers insights into the social implications of MySpace and early social media adoption. "The Filter Bubble: How the New Personalized Web is Changing What We Read and How We Think" by Eli Pariser. Analyzes the algorithms shaping social media, with applications to Facebook and Instagram. "Instagram: Visual Social Media Cultures" by Tama Leaver, Tim Highfield, and Crystal Abidin. Explores Instagram’s cultural impact, especially regarding visual culture, branding, and digital aesthetics. 3. Smartphones: From PDAs to Surveillance Machines (Apple/Google) Structure: Early PDA Technology : Introduce PDAs (e.g., Palm Pilot) and describe their primary role as organizational tools for early adopters and tech enthusiasts. Smartphone Innovation – Apple’s iPhone and Google’s Android : Outline the launch of the iPhone and the rise of Android, both of which expanded mobile tech from basic organizational functions to comprehensive media and lifestyle devices. Discuss how they popularized the app ecosystem, transforming smartphones into versatile personal devices. Mass Adoption and Data Tracking : Detail how Apple and Google began to collect vast amounts of user data, initially under the guise of personalization but ultimately as a foundation for surveillance capitalism. Ethical and Privacy Concerns : Analyze the concerns around constant tracking, data privacy, and user autonomy in the smartphone ecosystem. Conclude with the trade-off between innovation and surveillance. Recommended Reading: "The One Device: The Secret History of the iPhone" by Brian Merchant. Explores the iPhone’s development and its groundbreaking impact on tech. "Surveillance Capitalism: The Fight for a Human Future at the New Frontier of Power" by Shoshana Zuboff. Provides an in-depth look at how companies like Google and Apple use data collection for profit, impacting user privacy. "Always On: How the iPhone Unlocked the Anything-Anytime-Anywhere Future—and Locked Us In" by Brian X. Chen. Discusses the iPhone’s influence on the modern digital lifestyle and its role in our growing dependence on smartphones. "The Age of Surveillance Capitalism" by Shoshana Zuboff. Analyzes how Google and Apple transformed smartphones into tools for tracking and commodifying personal data. Case Studies in the Technology Lifecycle 4. YouTube: From User-Driven Platform to Commercialized Ecosystem Structure: Introduction to YouTube : Describe YouTube’s launch in 2005 as a platform for user-generated content, focused on simplicity and broad accessibility. Highlight early years of organic community growth, with YouTube’s “broadcast yourself” ethos resonating widely. Shift to Monetization and Algorithmic Control : After Google’s acquisition in 2006, examine how monetization features like the Partner Program, ads, and later algorithmic prioritization for ad revenue altered the platform’s goals and the types of content promoted. Impact on Community and Content Creation : Analyze how algorithm-driven content promotion reshaped YouTube’s creator community, pushing smaller creators to adopt commercial strategies and encouraging “clickbait” and ad-friendly content. Legacy and Challenges : Discuss how YouTube’s transformation reflects broader trends in the tech cycle and examine ongoing issues related to monetization pressure, content moderation, and user autonomy. Recommended Reading: "YouTube: Online Video and Participatory Culture" by Jean Burgess and Joshua Green. This book explores YouTube’s development from a user-centric platform to a major commercial entity, analyzing its cultural impact. "YouTube, Algorithmic Control, and the Commodification of Attention" by Taina Bucher. Journal of Communication (2018). A detailed look at how YouTube’s algorithms drive engagement and shape user behavior. "Watching YouTube: Extraordinary Videos by Ordinary People" by Michael Strangelove. Examines the transition of YouTube from a grassroots community to a corporate media giant. 5. Signal: Privacy-Centric Messaging Outside the Cycle Structure: Introduction to Signal : Outline Signal’s launch in 2014 as a privacy-first messaging app. Note its roots in cryptographic research and focus on end-to-end encryption. Non-Profit Model and Funding : Explain how Signal’s non-profit structure, funded by grants and donations, enables it to prioritize privacy and resist monetization pressures. Privacy-Driven Community and Growth : Examine how Signal’s commitment to privacy and data security attracted a user base focused on digital rights, especially during periods of concern about WhatsApp and Facebook Messenger. The Role as a Counter-Example : Discuss Signal’s influence on the tech landscape as a rare example of a large-scale tech platform that prioritizes user privacy over profit, examining potential limits and sustainability challenges in this model. Recommended Reading: "Signal: Messenger’s Billionaire Benefactor Has a Plan to Protect Your Data" – Forbes article by Parmy Olson. This article provides insight into Signal’s funding model and commitment to privacy. "Crypto Wars: Faked Deaths, Missing Billions and Industry Disruption" by Erica Stanford. This book discusses Signal in the broader context of the fight for privacy and data security. Signal Blog (signal.org/blog). Signal’s blog offers primary insight into its design philosophy, privacy practices, and updates from developers. 6. Wikipedia: The World’s Knowledge Repository Without Commercial Influence Structure: Introduction to Wikipedia : Describe Wikipedia’s founding in 2001 as a free, community-edited encyclopedia. Emphasize its core values of open access, transparency, and neutrality. Non-Profit Foundation and Funding : Explain how Wikipedia operates under the Wikimedia Foundation, relying on donations rather than ads, which helps keep the platform free from commercial pressures. Community Moderation and Open Knowledge Culture : Discuss Wikipedia’s unique, user-driven moderation system and how it fosters community involvement while upholding accuracy and neutrality standards. Legacy as an Open Knowledge Resource : Examine Wikipedia’s impact as a widely trusted, non-commercial resource. Reflect on its importance as a counterpoint to commercialized information platforms and its influence on knowledge-sharing in the digital age. Recommended Reading: "The Wikipedia Revolution: How a Bunch of Nobodies Created the World’s Greatest Encyclopedia" by Andrew Lih. This book explores Wikipedia’s unique model and cultural impact as a non-commercial knowledge resource. "Good Faith Collaboration: The Culture of Wikipedia" by Joseph Reagle. Analyzes Wikipedia’s community-based culture, collaboration practices, and neutrality challenges. Wikimedia Foundation Website (wikimediafoundation.org). Offers official information on Wikipedia’s governance, funding, and mission. Focus Areas: Technology Adoption Models : Examine theories like Rogers’ Diffusion of Innovations, contrasting it with the community-driven adoption model in your work. Cultural Shifts in Technology Use : Discuss research on how communities form around new technologies and how these communities are affected when the platform goes mainstream. Commercialization and Monetization : Explore studies on how commercialization changes user behavior, the platform's functionality, and the overall experience. Theoretical Framework Present a new model (or extend an existing one) to frame the cycle described in your work: The “Nerd-Initiated Cycle” (NIC) Hypothesis: A tech lifecycle that starts with niche communities and ends with mainstream adoption and commercialization. Describe this in academic terms, introducing each phase (creation, community growth, mainstream adoption, commercialization, decline). Methodology Describe the sources of your examples (e.g., IRC, social media platforms, tech hardware). Mention any potential qualitative analyses or case studies that demonstrate the cycle in action. Analysis Break down each stage into sections with academic rigor: Innovation and Creation : Discuss the impact of initial tech enthusiasm and ideation on early functionality. Community Growth : Examine how early adopters form a culture around the tech, often exclusive and built on mutual values. Mass Adoption : Analyze how non-tech users join, impacting the platform's culture and possibly diluting its original values. Commercialization and Monetization : Discuss the influence of corporations, revenue models, and advertising, and how these affect user behavior. Decline and Dissatisfaction : Describe the platform’s final state and explore the concept of a “new cycle” starting elsewhere. Case Studies or Examples Provide specific case studies like IRC, Facebook, or Apple products, analyzing each phase within the cycle in these examples. Discussion Discuss broader implications, such as: The sustainability of tech communities in a commercialized world. Potential alternatives for breaking the cycle. How innovators might sustain culture despite commercialization. Conclusion Summarize the cyclical model and its impacts, suggesting potential avenues for future tech developments to sustain their original ethos. References Cite literature on technology adoption, commercialization, and user behavior to establish an academic foundation.

docs.text: Presentation: The Wheel Turns – Analyzing the Lifecycle of Innovation, Adoption, and Commercialization Slide 1: Opening – The Old Internet Title: My Favorite Game and Website Talking Points : "I want to talk to you about my favorite video game. Moonstone for the Amiga." "But actually, I want to tell you about my favorite website: The Moonstone Tavern." Describe the “Old Internet” feel: No ads, cookie popups, notifications, or data-gathering. Emphasize: “It’s what websites used to be—repositories of information, built by the people who cared enough to dedicate their time to them.” Transition : "But today, we’re going to explore what changed, why it changed, and how it fits into the larger cycle of tech." Slide 2: Introduction to the Cyclical Nature of Tech Title: Understanding the Cyclical Nature of Technology Talking Points : Introduce the idea of a recurring “tech lifecycle” with phases: innovation, adoption, commercialization, and decline. Discuss the cultural shifts and commercialization impacts within this cycle. Outline the three case studies: IRC, Social Media, and Smartphones. Slide 3: Overview of the Lifecycle Phases Title: Phases of the Technology Lifecycle Talking Points : Breakdown of phases: Innovation and Creation , Community Growth , Mass Adoption , Commercialization , Decline and Dissatisfaction . Briefly describe each phase’s relevance across all case studies. Slide 4: IRC – The Birth of Online Community Title: IRC: From Niche Platform to Obsolescence Talking Points : Origins of IRC (Internet Relay Chat) as a pioneer for tech communities in the 1980s and 90s. Appeal to tech enthusiasts and the creation of exclusive, tech-centered subcultures. Note its lack of commercialization and how this maintained its niche appeal but limited its longevity. Slide 5: IRC – Community Growth and Decline Title: IRC’s Legacy and Cultural Impact Talking Points : How IRC fostered collaborative communities and a DIY ethos. User-generated features and commands that became cultural hallmarks. Explain IRC’s decline as user-friendly, commercialized chat services appeared. Slide 6: Social Media – MySpace to Facebook to Instagram Title: Social Media Evolution: MySpace, Facebook, and Instagram Talking Points : MySpace’s rise as an unregulated, creativity-driven social platform. Transition to Facebook: from exclusive, data-driven platform to mainstream adoption. The rise of Instagram: mobile-friendly, photo-centric, and eventually acquired by Facebook. Slide 7: Social Media – Commercialization and Privacy Trade-offs Title: From Innovation to Data-Driven Commercialization Talking Points : Facebook and Instagram’s shift from user-centric to ad-centric models. docs.text: Cycles of Digital Community: A Sociotechnical Model of the Rise and Fall of Social Platforms from IRC to TikTok Abstract This paper proposes a sociotechnical lifecycle model for online social platforms, consisting of seven recurring stages: Inception, Early Adoption, Mass Appeal, Commercialization, Stagnation, Migration, and Abandonment. Drawing on case studies from IRC, Reddit, Facebook, and Twitter, as well as theoretical frameworks in media ecology, platform capitalism, and digital sociology, we argue that the collapse of online communities stems not only from economic and technical pressures but from social dynamics—especially the effects of anonymity, identity rigidity, and exclusionary cultural behavior. The persistence of these patterns across decades suggests a structural phenomenon worthy of deeper academic inquiry. 1. Introduction Hypothesis: Social platforms follow a predictable lifecycle that can be observed historically and structurally. Overview of prior scholarship: danah boyd (social network sites, teen engagement) José van Dijck (cultural history of platforms) Nick Srnicek (platform capitalism) Introduce the sociotechnical lifecycle: 7 stages. 2. Historical Foundations: IRC as Proto-Social Media IRC origins (1988), cultural epicenter for early digital sociality. Early adoption: niche, tech-heavy, identity-masked. Migration and commercialization at odds: e.g. Freenode becomes a support hub, but staffed by impatient, often exclusionary tech culture. Decline due to rigidity, user hostility, and inability to evolve UX. Source examples: Reid, "Electropolis" (1991) Hafner, Where Wizards Stay Up Late (1996) 3. Lifecycle Model The 7-Stage Sociotechnical Lifecycle: Inception Ideological, experimental, limited audience. IRC, LiveJournal, early Twitter. Early Adoption Tight-knit, culture-defining communities form. High trust, low friction. Mass Appeal Exponential growth; media attention. Shift from community -> audience. Commercialization Ads, influencer culture, venture capital pressure. Algorithmic feeds, data harvesting. Stagnation UX declines, feature bloat, institutional rigidity. Community splits and tensions grow. Migration Power users depart; parallel ecosystems rise. Examples: Discord, Mastodon, Substack. Abandonment / Ghost Town Bots, spam, diehards remain. MySpace, Google+, Vine. 4. Social Decay: Hostility, Rigidity, and the Toxic Core Online Disinhibition Effect (Suler, 2004): Anonymity unleashes toxic behavior. Cultural Lock-in: Users resist changes; identity becomes tied to outdated systems. IRC as case study: Freenode's degeneration due to support-hostility. Example of "support paradox"—most technical users are least empathetic. Disability and neurodivergence: Platforms act as social prosthetics for users with limited offline engagement. These users resist migration and become guardians of legacy culture. Ethical framing: Use "neurodivergent," avoid pathologizing. Cite insiders where possible. 5. Causal Mechanisms Economic incentives: Attention economy and surveillance capitalism (Srnicek). Governance and moderation failure (Gillespie). Fragmentation and identity collapse. Psychological/social fatigue: social media as a performance. 6. Resilience and Exceptions? Can any platform escape the cycle? Federation, decentralization (Mastodon, Matrix). Co-op and Web3 attempts. Challenges: UX, discoverability, fragmentation. Nostalgia as a trap: early adopters often rewrite history. Suggest need for sustainable governance and community ethics. 7. Conclusion Lifecycle is a pattern, not a destiny—but few platforms course-correct. Suggest future work: Longitudinal studies of user migration. Neurodivergent experience in digital communities. Comparative analysis of federated vs centralized platforms. References (Suggested Core Reading) Suler, John. "The Online Disinhibition Effect." CyberPsychology & Behavior , 2004. van Dijck, José. The Culture of Connectivity , 2013. Srnicek, Nick. Platform Capitalism , 2017. McLuhan, Marshall. Understanding Media , 1964. Gillespie, Tarleton. Custodians of the Internet , 2018. Boyd, danah. It's Complicated , 2014. Ellison & Boyd. "Social Network Sites: Definition, History, and Scholarship", 2007. Reid, Elizabeth. “Electropolis: Communication and Community on Internet Relay Chat.” (1991). Zittrain, Jonathan. “The Generative Internet.” Harvard Law Review , 2006.

docs.text: Diffusion of Innovations — Everett M. Rogers — 1962 — Book — Explains how new ideas spread through adopter groups over time; gives you the “adoption curve” backbone you can extend into “what happens when commercialization hits.” Platform Capitalism — Nick Srnicek — 2016 — Book — Argues platforms structurally trend toward data extraction, monopoly, and rent-seeking; perfect for explaining why “profit pressure” isn’t accidental, it’s baked in. Understanding Media: The Extensions of Man — Marshall McLuhan — 1964 — Book — “The medium is the message” framing: the form of media reshapes society; supports your claim that algorithmic/monetized platform changes transform culture regardless of content. Amusing Ourselves to Death: Public Discourse in the Age of Show Business — Neil Postman — 1985 — Book — Shows how media optimized for entertainment corrodes public discourse; ports cleanly to feeds/engagement metrics and helps you argue degradation is a predictable media effect. The Age of Surveillance Capitalism — Shoshana Zuboff — 2019 — Book — Documents how behavioral data becomes the core profit engine and incentives shift toward prediction/control; directly supports your “information becomes extraction” thesis. Networks of Power: Electrification in Western Society, 1880–1930 — Thomas P. Hughes — 1993 — Book — Classic large-scale sociotechnical history showing tech systems evolve with institutions/culture/power; useful for grounding your cycle as “systems behavior,” not just internet drama. The Social Construction of Technological Systems — Wiebe E. Bijker, Thomas P. Hughes, Trevor Pinch (eds.) — 1987 — Edited book — Foundational STS/SCOT collection arguing technologies are shaped by social groups and politics; gives you academic cover for “platform outcomes are socially produced.” We Have Never Been Modern — Bruno Latour — 1991 (English translation 1993) — Book — Core STS argument that “social” and “technical” are inseparable networks; helps you frame platforms as hybrid systems whose “decay” is socio-economic, not purely technical. It’s Complicated: The Social Lives of Networked Teens — danah boyd — 2014 — Book — Empirical, sociologically rigorous analysis of how social platforms change behavior as they scale; excellent for grounding “community degradation” in observed user dynamics rather than nostalgia. The Filter Bubble: What the Internet Is Hiding from You — Eli Pariser — 2011 — Book — Early, influential account of algorithmic personalization fragmenting shared reality; supports your claim that commercialization reshapes information quality, not just delivery. The Culture of Connectivity — José van Dijck — 2013 — Book — Analyzes social media platforms as socio-economic systems, not neutral tools; bridges your cultural argument with platform political economy. YouTube: Online Video and Participatory Culture — Jean Burgess & Joshua Green — 2009 — Book — Strong historical account of YouTube’s transition from participatory culture to commercial platform; anchors your YouTube case study in peer-reviewed scholarship. Custodians of the Internet — Tarleton Gillespie — 2018 — Book — Definitive work on moderation, governance, and platform power; excellent for explaining how degradation is enforced and normalized at scale. Understanding Media — Marshall McLuhan — 1964 — Book — You already implicitly rely on this; your file correctly treats McLuhan as a conceptual ancestor rather than a novelty citation. Platform Capitalism — Nick Srnicek — 2016 — Book — Already core to your argument; your usage is correct and disciplined, not cargo-culted. The Age of Surveillance Capitalism — Shoshana Zuboff — 2019 — Book — You deploy this appropriately in the smartphone and social media sections; it cleanly supports your causal mechanism. Core Time cost: 3–5 hours per book Here you read with intent: Enough to understand how the argument is built Enough to not misrepresent them Enough to confidently say “I extend this” Still not cover-to-cover. Still selective. List Diffusion of Innovations — Everett M. Rogers Platform Capitalism — Nick Srnicek The Age of Surveillance Capitalism — Shoshana Zuboff Understanding Media: The Extensions of Man — Marshall McLuhan Amusing Ourselves to Death — Neil Postman The Culture of Connectivity — José van Dijck It’s Complicated: The Social Lives of Networked Teens — danah boyd The Filter Bubble — Eli Pariser Custodians of the Internet — Tarleton Gillespie YouTube: Online Video and Participatory Culture — Jean Burgess & Joshua Green Networks of Power: Electrification in Western Society, 1880–1930 — Thomas P. Hughes The Social Construction of Technological Systems — Wiebe E. Bijker, Thomas P. Hughes, Trevor Pinch (eds.) Citation-level reading (most books) Time cost: 30–60 minutes You read: Introduction Table of contents 1–2 key chapters Conclusion Your goal is to extract: The core claim The language they use 1–2 quotable ideas That’s it. You are not marrying the book. You’re borrowing vocabulary. This will apply to 10–12 of the 16. Easily. docs.text: The dead internet theory suggests that the internet now consists mainly of automatically generated content that is consumed automatically by bot activity designed to simulate an active and vibrant community. The internet cannot be regulated by standard means It cannot be controlled in standard ways. Any attempts to outlaw anything just mean that innocent people will stop using that particular thing. Criminals will not stop. If we outlaw encryption, consumers will have bad encryption but criminals will not. Similarly, if we outlaw AI, the only uses of AI become criminal uses. This doesn't mean they stop. Deepfakes are now so advanced that they're fooling people into handing over money. Voices can be synthesized and generated. Images are not far behind, if they don't exist already. Dead Internet Theory might actually be a glimpse of the future. In a case where the internet becomes problematically difficult for the average person to navigate. Might create a return to meatspace, where physical interaction is the best way to ensure veracity. Useful Link: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dead_Internet_theory Read. Write paper. Publish paper. Then make presentation. Then write fiction!

docs.text: Bot farms are interesting places to start. Basically a pre-cursor to the dead internet. Asks the question can people's behaviour be influenced and manipulated online. They've shaped our culture for more than a decade without people realising. Get the bot farm art discuss. Musk Twitter Bullshit Musk has been setting the stage to abandon the deal since just weeks after he signed the agreement, claiming that Twitter released misleading stats about the prevalence of spam bots on its platform. https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2023/sep/09/x-twitter-bots-republican-primary-debate-tweets-increase Inside Click Farms - ChatGPT recommendations https://www.fiverr.com/gigs/twitter-bot https://cheq.ai/blog/8-bizarre-click-farms-discovered-and-how-they-worked/ https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IwjCAM0XxzE&ab_channel=FRANCE24English https://wiredspace.wits.ac.za/server/api/core/bitstreams/0e06ad71-37b6-4d11-8ee2-873a1bc15a61/content Challenging Truth and Trust Wiki quotes “Although click farm services violate many social media user policies, there are no government regulations that render them illegal.” – WIKIPEDIA “Click farms are usually located in developing countries, such as China, India, Nepal, Sri Lanka, Egypt, Indonesia, the Philippines, and Bangladesh.” – WIKIPEDIA “In Thailand in June 2017, a click farm was discovered with hundreds of mobile phones and several hundred thousand SIM cards.” – WIKIPEDIA Find a source for them Pics Chen San-yuan from Taiwan 17 phones per row, 3 rows, 2 stations visible, presumably 4 opposite. Why phones? One comment I hear a lot is "Fake. Why would they use phones. You can spin up 8,301,123 virtual machines." It's not really about the number of devices, it's about the connections. Let's say you have a server, you have a couple of NICs, you have a handful of connections. If all your traffic comes from here, it's easy to flag. Cue the phones. The important part is not the device but the SIM card slot. “In Thailand in June 2017, a click farm was discovered with hundreds of mobile phones and several hundred thousand SIM cards." That's the operative matter, the number of unique connections you can spawn. This is what makes you hard to track. Business Model? Cost of phone in China == the cheapest Chinese Android-based smartphones at just under 1000RMB up to your regular 5000-6000RMB Nokias For high performance-price ratio phones, like Red Mi, Red Mi Note and Huawei Honour Youth Edition is often below 100 USD, like Red Mi 5A: 4-core Snapdragon425, 2/16G, is about 60 USD $10 for 1000 clicks Time per click + device switch: 1 + 1 seconds. Approx to 50 clicks / minute for easy math. Need 20 stations to get 1000 unique clicks without sim switching. 10 with dual sim. Cost of setup per station == cost of phone * number of phones == 70*51 == 3570 Cost of location setup == cost of station * number of required stations + misc hardware & cabling costs == 3570 * 20 + 1000 == 71400 + 1000 == 72400 Output of 1000 clicks per 2 minutes. Max income == output price * conversion to 10 minutes * conversion to hours == 10 * 5 * 6 == $300 / h input https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_China Minimum wage in Beijing == 25.3 == 3,49 United States Dollar Input - (wage * 20) - operator wage 300 - (3.49 * 20) - 6 == 224.2 ROI == setup costs / net income == 323 ROI_days == ROI / working hours per day == 323 / 8 == 37.5 Return on setup costs in approximately 1 month and 1 week. Costs can be reduced by dual simming, turns setup costs to approx half, reduces wage requirements, etc bringing ROI to 2 weeks. This assume maximal output. What this can do. Milgram Experiment Campus binge drinking - NIU reduced by 44% - Check Fox's article. Hotel Towel Reuse - People want to conform, and everyone knows this. Marketers take advantage of this. They lead our trends - Spotify listens can be bought and manipulated - Do you trust the music industry? By the time the click farmer gets to the last phone, the first phone is ready to go again. Or just set the album going. Your attention is already being purchased and redirected. You are already being manipulated. Social media - The Social Experiment - The Social Network

docs.text: Thoughts: What portion of the internet is the following kinds of traffic: 1) B2B - Everything from logistics to data transfer to whatever. Often audomated. No direct inpact on end users. 2) B2C - Businesses selling things to clients, or interacting with them in any way. Data gathering. 3) C2C - Person to person communication. How much does 3 bleed into 2? Do people interact with one another at akll without feeding third parties their data to sell? Signal as example of no data bleed. Wikipedia as example of no data bleed GPT attempt (20250203 - ChatGPT o3-mini) Breakdown of Internet Traffic by B2B, B2C, and C2C Determining exact percentages of global Internet traffic by the categories B2B , B2C , and C2C is extremely challenging. After reviewing available research and industry reports, I couldn’t locate any rigorous, peer‐reviewed studies or industry analyses that directly break down overall traffic into these segments. Challenges in Defining and Measuring the Categories Blurry Boundaries Multiple Functions: Many online services and platforms serve multiple functions simultaneously. For example, social media platforms can be used for both: C2C: Person-to-person interactions. B2C: Brand–consumer engagement. Mixed Elements: Websites often incorporate both automated (B2B) and consumer-facing (B2C/C2C) elements. Different Measurement Frameworks Application-Based Metrics: Most studies and reports (e.g., Cisco’s Visual Networking Index , Akamai’s State of the Internet Reports , Sandvine’s Global Internet Phenomena Reports ) break down traffic by: Application type (e.g., video streaming, web browsing, gaming) Protocols Device types These metrics differ from the business/consumer relationship focus of B2B, B2C, and C2C. Evolving Ecosystem Changing Models: The ways in which businesses, consumers, and individuals interact online are continually evolving. New models that blend aspects of B2B and B2C or incorporate commercial transactions into peer-to-peer services complicate any attempt at a clean segmentation. What We Do Know Enterprise Traffic (Often Associated with B2B) Automated & Enterprise Data: Significant automated and enterprise traffic includes data transfers, cloud services, and machine-to-machine communications. Data Volume vs. Categorization: Although enterprise services (e.g., cloud data center traffic) account for a large share of global IP traffic, this does not directly translate into a “B2B” percentage in the sense described here. Consumer Traffic (B2C/C2C) Consumer-Centric Applications: Much published data focuses on consumer-centric applications: Video streaming Social media Online gaming Shared Traffic Sources: For example, Akamai’s reports note that video streaming makes up a substantial portion of Internet data volume. However, it is often unclear whether such traffic is strictly: B2C: Business-to-consumer (e.g., streaming services) C2C: User-generated content sharing Conclusion No Definitive Percentages Available: There is currently no authoritative source that assigns clear percentage values to global Internet traffic segmented by B2B , B2C , and C2C . Any attempt to do so would require making several assumptions about the definitions used and would likely vary based on those definitions. Recommendations for Further Research Commissioning a Custom Study: Organizations such as Gartner, IDC, or Forrester may conduct market segmentation studies that touch on these distinctions within certain contexts. Internal Traffic Analysis: If you have access to network traffic analytics for a specific service or platform, you may classify flows based on known endpoints or application types. Sector-Specific Reports: While global data might be unavailable, sector-specific studies (e.g., in e-commerce, social media, or enterprise networking) might provide clues to the split in those domains. Sources Consulted Cisco Visual Networking Index (VNI): Cisco VNI Reports These reports provide insights into overall traffic trends, but not by B2B/B2C/C2C. Akamai State of the Internet Reports: Akamai’s State of the Internet These reports focus on application types and usage patterns. Sandvine Global Internet Phenomena Reports: Sandvine Phenomena Reports These reports analyze traffic by application and protocol rather than by business/consumer interaction types. Approaches to Estimating Internet Traffic Percentages Below are several ideas and methods that might help in estimating or determining the percentages of global Internet traffic segmented by B2B , B2C , and C2C . 1. Define Clear Classifications and Criteria Operational Definitions: Establish precise definitions for what qualifies as B2B, B2C, and C2C traffic. For example, determine whether automated machine-to-machine communications (e.g., API calls) should always be classified as B2B, or if certain consumer interactions (like transactions on a marketplace) belong to B2C or C2C. Metadata and Tagging: Use metadata such as domain registration details, known IP ranges, or SSL certificate information to tag traffic flows by their likely category. 2. Leverage Data from ISPs and Network Operators Traffic Analysis: Collaborate with large Internet Service Providers (ISPs) or network operators who have access to broad traffic data. They can use techniques like Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) or flow analysis (using NetFlow, sFlow, etc.) to categorize traffic. Anonymized Datasets: Participate in projects or studies that aggregate anonymized traffic data from various networks to reveal patterns corresponding to different categories. 3. Utilize Data from Content Delivery Networks (CDNs) and Cloud Providers Traffic Volume Data: Companies such as Akamai, Cloudflare, or Amazon Web Services (AWS) possess detailed traffic data. Analyzing these datasets in aggregate might allow for inference between business and consumer traffic. API and Service Analytics: Analyze internal analytics from platforms that serve both B2B and B2C clients (e.g., e-commerce platforms or social media sites) to differentiate user behaviors. 4. Machine Learning and Automated Classification Traffic Pattern Recognition: Develop machine learning models that analyze traffic patterns. Train these models to differentiate between types of traffic based on usage patterns, protocols, destination IPs, and other features. Natural Language Processing (NLP): Apply NLP techniques on application-layer data (like HTTP headers, URLs, or user-agent strings) to assist in classifying traffic when sufficient training data is available. 5. Surveys and Self-Reported Data Industry Surveys: Conduct surveys or studies among businesses, ISPs, and platform providers to understand how they classify their own traffic. Academic Research: Partner with academic institutions to design research studies that combine technical analysis with survey data to validate traffic categorizations. 6. Cross-Referencing Multiple Data Sources Correlation Analysis: Use multiple independent data sources (e.g., ISP data, CDN logs, enterprise analytics) to cross-validate findings. Time-Series Analysis: Monitor changes over time to determine if trends (such as increased mobile traffic or video streaming) correlate with shifts in the distribution between B2B, B2C, and C2C activities. Challenges to Consider Encryption and Privacy: With the increase in encryption (e.g., HTTPS) and privacy measures, inspecting the content of the traffic becomes more challenging without infringing on user trust or legal boundaries. Dynamic and Hybrid Models: Many platforms blur the lines between these categories (for example, a social media platform can exhibit both B2C and C2C characteristics), making neat separation difficult. Scale and Variability: The vast scale of global Internet traffic and variability across regions and industries adds complexity to any attempt at segmentation. Conclusion While gathering precise percentages is challenging due to technical and definitional hurdles, combining network analysis techniques with business data and machine learning could provide useful estimates or trends. A collaborative, multi-source approach—possibly involving industry partnerships or academic research—will likely be necessary to arrive at reliable figures. Rough Estimate of Internet Traffic Percentages Below is a speculative breakdown with the strong disclaimer that these numbers are purely conjectural and should not be taken as authoritative. B2B: ~35% of global Internet traffic B2C: ~45% of global Internet traffic C2C: ~20% of global Internet traffic Important Caveats Definitions Matter: These percentages depend heavily on how you define each category. Many services and platforms serve multiple roles simultaneously (e.g., a social media platform might have both B2C and C2C elements). Data Overlap: Automated communications (often categorized as B2B) and consumer interactions (B2C/C2C) can overlap, making clear segmentation difficult. Evolving Internet Usage: With rapid changes in online behavior, mobile usage, and IoT devices, the balance between these categories may shift over time. Lack of Rigorous Data: Currently, no comprehensive, peer-reviewed studies or industry reports break down global Internet traffic strictly by B2B, B2C, and C2C. These figures are an educated guess based on known trends (e.g., enterprise traffic, video streaming, social media usage) and should be treated as such. If you need reliable data, a custom study or analysis using detailed traffic logs from multiple sources (ISPs, CDNs, etc.) would be necessary. Rationale Behind the Rough Estimate The rough estimates of B2B ~35% , B2C ~45% , and C2C ~20% were derived based on several factors and assumptions: 1. Observed Traffic Trends Enterprise Traffic (B2B): Volume of Automated Data: Enterprises often generate large volumes of automated data, including API calls, data transfers, and communications between servers. Cloud and Data Center Traffic: A significant portion of global Internet traffic comes from cloud services and data centers, which are primarily B2B in nature. Consumer-Centric Traffic (B2C & C2C): High-Volume Applications: Consumer-facing applications such as video streaming, social media, and online gaming are known to drive massive amounts of traffic. User Engagement Patterns: Platforms like social media serve both business-to-consumer (B2C) functions (e.g., advertising, brand engagement) and consumer-to-consumer (C2C) interactions (e.g., messaging, sharing content). 2. Market and Industry Insights Historical Data: Previous reports from organizations like Akamai, Cisco, and Sandvine have shown that consumer content (including streaming and interactive media) makes up a substantial share of overall traffic. This helped guide the B2C and C2C estimates. Overlap Consideration: Recognizing that many platforms blur the lines (for example, a social media site can have both B2C and C2C elements), an educated guess was made that B2C might be slightly higher than C2C. 3. Estimation Approach Assumptions: The B2B component, while significant, is often more automated and less visible to the average consumer, but still represents a substantial part of traffic. B2C interactions are typically more data-intensive, especially with the prevalence of video and interactive content. C2C traffic, while important, is presumed to be lower in aggregate volume compared to structured B2C content. Lack of Direct Data: Because there is no single comprehensive source that breaks down traffic into these exact categories, the estimates are based on correlating known trends in data usage, enterprise versus consumer data flows, and the evolving nature of online interactions. 4. Evolving Internet Landscape Technological Changes: The rapid growth in mobile devices, IoT, and increased encryption all influence traffic patterns. These factors suggest that while the general proportions might hold, they are subject to change as technologies evolve. Blended Usage Patterns: Many modern platforms are hybrid in nature, which means that any numerical estimates must be understood as rough approximations rather than precise measurements. In Summary: The conclusions stem from a combination of observed industry trends, historical data, and assumptions based on the nature of enterprise versus consumer data flows. Given the complex and overlapping nature of internet traffic, these estimates are an educated guess rather than definitive figures. The following suggests that 50% or more of internet traffic by volume is video. Based on mobile traffic: https://www.statista.com/statistics/1312357/global-data-traffic-by-content-type/#:~:text=Global%20data%20traffic%201H%202021%2C%20by%20category&text=In%20the%20first%20half%20of,0.31%20percent%20of%20traffic%20worldwide. https://www.ncta.com/whats-new/report-where-does-the-majority-of-internet-traffic-come https://www.sandvine.com/phenomena This makes a "by data volume" comparison somewhat useless. Engagement metrics are far more important. How do we determine that? Data Consumed / Hours == Data Engagement Metric Not sure if this is a helpful method? Fuck? Might not actually matter. We don't care about the B2B stuff. Might be interesting: Seems to be a year-spanning data-set. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41597-024-03927-4 Basically it's a hard question to answer. By volume, it seems obvious that people are the largest consumer, with B2B being a small subset of data. Video transfer is the largest, followed by social media and gaming, when it came to mobile use. Which suggests B2C is the largest portion, doubly so if we blur C2C into B2C. So I think it's safe to say we're looking at a majority hostile internet.

docs.text: Bots on bluesky Regarding bots on bluesky: One thing I've noticed is people's profiles is this trend of writing "No DMs, they're always scams," or some version of. Users can easily disable DMs. The settings are "Allow DMs from... Everyone / People you follow / No one." So writing "No DMs" is unnecessary, and... based on the times I see this type of profile... I'm starting to suspect it's a strategy used by bots to manipulate trust levels. Announcing that you're trying to avoid scam/spammers is a great way to convince others that you're not a scam/spam account. But this person here is following 174.1k other accounts (and I literally just watched it jump from 173.9k a few seconds ago). Only 26.6k follow back, which indicates the number of people (or bots) that automatically follow back when someone follows them. And there is no link on their profile, despite advertising their "small creative shop" in their profile. https://bsky.app/profile/crimeajewel.bsky.social They're posts are almost entirely somewhat bad pics of jewelry. And they NEVER reply to people's comments on their posts. Nor do they like/heart comments made to their posts. Nor do they every comment on anyone else's posts. They post every hour or every half-hour, in short segments of a few hours at a time. So... To me, this account screams bot. And the "No DMs!"
